desc: Inside zone blocking to one side while the QB reads the backside end for a potential keep. If the end crashes on the RB path, QB pulls and runs outside the end. A zone-read with an inverted path.

name: Run vs. Press Technique
desc: OL works their run-blocking assignments against a press shade technique from a 1-technique defensive tackle. Develop the double-team footwork and hand placement to move a player out of a tight alignment.

name: Backfield Mesh Timing
desc: QB and RB work the handoff mesh in isolation — correct depth, soft hands on the mesh, and RB's eyes up through the exchange. Every handoff must be clean; fumbles on exchanges are tracked.

name: Fourth-Down Run Package
desc: Run dedicated fourth-down short-yardage runs — QB sneak with guards helping, power from a jumbo set, and a designed QB keeper. Defense plays live. Conversion rate tracked by play.

name: Punt Block Fake Run
desc: Install a fake punt with a direct-snap run — up-back takes the snap and runs an assigned gap while the punter sells the punt motion. Defense must respect both threats.

name: Read Option Mesh
desc: QB and RB work the zone-read mesh at full speed — QB rides the mesh point, makes the give or keep decision, and either hands off or pulls cleanly. Mesh timing is drilled 20+ reps per session.

name: Goal Line Power Series
desc: Run the full goal-line power series from the 1-yard line — QB sneak, off-tackle, dive, and power sweep. Every play run twice from each hash. Defense plays 8-man front.

name: Wide Zone vs. Odd Front
desc: Run outside zone specifically against an odd-front defense — 3-4 or 5-2. OL adjusts reach and combo assignments. RB reads the edge and either turns the corner or cuts back to the B-gap.

name: RB Pre-Snap Alignment
desc: RBs practice lining up correctly based on formation and play call — pistol, I-formation, offset I, and singleback. Every alignment communicates something to the defense; RBs must be disciplined.

name: Combo Block to Second Level
desc: Guard and center work a double-team on a nose tackle, then one releases to block the linebacker. Practiced 20+ reps with a focus on the timing of the release — leave only when the DL is moving.

name: Toss Lead Right & Left
desc: QB tosses to the RB sweeping both directions while the FB leads as the kick-out blocker. Work the toss timing, FB path, and WR crack block on each side.

name: RB Spin Move Drill
desc: RB works the spin move in isolation — dip the shoulder, spin 360 degrees, and burst out of the spin at full speed. Work both left and right spins 10 times each.

name: Hurdle & Jump-Cut Drill
desc: RB runs through a course with low bags representing defenders. Must hurdle the bag or jump-cut around it on each rep. Develops the jump-cut and hurdle instincts needed for big-play potential.

name: Under-Center Handoff Timing
desc: QB takes snaps under center and hands off to running backs on inside and outside runs. Focus on the QB's reverse pivot, mesh depth, and clean exchange without any fumble risk.

name: Run Blocking Film Review
desc: OL watches film of their last game's run-blocking performance together. Identify missed combo assignments, poor pad level, and missed cut-off blocks. Each player identifies one correction.

name: Inside-Outside Zone Combo Script
desc: Run both inside and outside zone in the same scripted period — alternating calls. Defense must stay disciplined with their gap assignments regardless of which zone concept hits their gap.

name: Counter vs. Backside Pursuit
desc: Run the counter specifically to challenge backside pursuit — defense must not give up the cutback while also defending the counter. Tests discipline and gap-sound defense against misdirection.

name: Full Run vs. Defense Evaluation
desc: Comprehensive run-game evaluation — 15 plays from the full run game script against live defense. Both sides graded. Determine which runs are working and which need repair before game week.

name: Trap vs. Slanting Defense
desc: Run the trap specifically against a slanting defensive line — the most challenging trap assignment. Guard must identify the slant direction and adjust the pull path to stay on target.

name: Winning on First Down
desc: Dedicated first-down scripted period — all plays designed for positive first-down yardage. Both offense and defense compete with a clear standard: 4+ yards is a win for the offense.

name: T-Formation Dive Series
desc: Run the classic T-formation dive series — QB under center, two backs, full-house formation. Dive left, dive right, fullback blast. Develops under-center timing and classic gap-blocking fundamentals.

name: Stretch Play vs. Feathering DE
desc: Run outside zone against a defensive end who feathers — retreats and maintains outside leverage. OL must reach or cut him to open the edge. RB must be patient and find the cut-back lane.

name: Isolation on the Linebacker
desc: ISO play with a FB lead — OL blocks the down linemen, FB leads up on the linebacker. Run this specifically to attack a slow or undersized linebacker. Full live reps with defensive movement.

name: RB Catch Ball in Traffic
desc: RB runs a wheel route or flat route and catches the ball with a linebacker closing from behind. Develops the courage and hand strength needed to make catches with contact arriving immediately.

name: Sprint Draw vs. 3-4
desc: Run the sprint draw against a 3-4 defense — OL sets up as pass protection, QB sprints to his set position and hands off on a delay. Attacks the 3-4 when outside backers rush wide.

name: H-Back Motion into Run
desc: H-back goes in motion and becomes a lead blocker or kicks out the end on an edge run. QB times the handoff to the RB as the H-back passes the formation. Develops full-motion run timing.

name: RB Pick-Up the Blitz
desc: RB works picking up blitzing linebackers and safeties — identify the blitzer, step to the threat, and absorb the hit. Protect the QB at all costs. Tracked by successful protections per session.

name: Full Run-Game Scrimmage
desc: Full run-game scrimmage period — all rushing plays from the full playbook, live defense, and tracked results. Film every rep. This is the final evaluation of the run game before game week execution.

name: Sideline Catch Technique
desc: Receivers work catching the ball on the boundary sideline — one foot in-bounds for college, two feet for pros. Toes drag, hips toward the field, and eyes track the ball all the way through the catch.

name: Back-Shoulder Catch Drill
desc: QB intentionally throws behind the receiver's back shoulder. WR must feel the ball's location, adjust, and catch it without turning fully. Develops spatial awareness and the ability to adjust in flight.

name: Over-the-Shoulder Deep Ball
desc: WR runs a go route at full speed and catches a 40-yard bomb over the outside shoulder without breaking stride. Eyes track early, reach back, and secure before making contact with the ground.

name: Low Ball & Scoop Technique
desc: Balls thrown at the receiver's shoe tops — scoop with soft hands, palms up, pinkies together. Never let the ball reach the body. Drill 20 consecutive low catches to build the muscle memory.

name: Run After Catch Open Field
desc: Receiver catches a short pass and navigates 4 cones simulating defenders in open space — juke, plant, and accelerate to the end zone. Develops YAC instincts and open-field vision.

name: Slot Receiver Quick Game
desc: Slot receivers run their quick-game menu — speed out, slant, spot route, and flat — with a QB in a rapid-fire sequence. The slot must be clean off the line and precise at the break point.

name: One-Handed Catch Drill
desc: Receivers catch balls thrown to their right or left hand only — both hands behind the back. Develops independent hand strength and the ability to make a one-handed adjustment catch in tight coverage.

name: Post Route Deep Ball Timing
desc: WR runs a post route and the QB throws 35-45 yards to the post window between the safeties. WR tracks the ball over the inside shoulder and catches at the highest point. Timing off the break must be precise.

name: Contested Red Zone Catch
desc: WR and CB compete for a fade or back-shoulder throw in the end zone. WR must use body positioning, high-point technique, and physicality to secure the ball against tight coverage.

name: JUGS Machine Volume Catching
desc: Receivers catch 50+ balls from the JUGS machine at varying speeds and heights. Volume catching builds hand reliability. Every drop is noted — zero drops is the standard.

name: After-Contact Catch Drill
desc: A defender hits the receiver as the ball arrives. The receiver must absorb the contact and still secure the catch. Builds physical toughness and concentration through contact over the middle.

name: Double Move Route Running
desc: WR runs a route with two breaks — in-and-up, out-and-up, or post-corner. The first move must sell hard enough to get the defender to commit. Full speed against a live DB.

name: Dig Route Mechanics
desc: WR runs the dig (in route) at 12-15 yards — vertical push, plant the outside foot, and break sharply inside. Head snaps to the QB on the break. Ball arrives before the defender can close.

name: Skinny Post Route
desc: WR runs a skinny post — a narrower post angle that splits the safeties and wins over the top of a single-high safety. Develops the angle variation needed to attack different coverage shells.

name: Route Running Against Press
desc: WR works a full set of releases against press man coverage — speed release, inside release, and push-go. Each release designed to beat the CB's hands at the line of scrimmage.

name: Slot Fade Route
desc: Slot receiver runs a fade from the inside alignment toward the sideline — a condensed fade. QB targets the receiver as he drives to the boundary. Red zone weapon against press man.

name: TE Blocking & Route Combo
desc: TE practices their dual role — run the play as a run blocker, then immediately transition to a route runner on a pass. Reinforces the TE's ability to hide the run-pass intention from the defense.

name: Wide Receiver Stalk Block
desc: WRs practice stalk blocking on corners and safeties — approach at controlled speed, get into the defender's chest, mirror their movements, and seal them away from the ball carrier.

name: Catch & Protect Versus Jam
desc: Receiver runs a short route and catches the ball with a defender attempting a jam at the catch point. Must secure the ball, tuck, and protect through the contact.

name: Tunnel Vision Catch Drill
desc: Receiver runs a route through traffic — two defenders with shields on either side — eyes locked on the ball the entire way. Builds the mental focus needed to catch in a congested area.

name: Hitch Route Footwork
desc: WR runs a 5-yard hitch — three steps full speed, plant, and pop back. The return to the QB on the hitch must be sharp and timed to the QB's release. No drifting; stay on the route stem.

name: Full Route Tree Circuit
desc: WR runs each route from the standard route tree — 1 through 9 — in rotation with a QB. One route per rep, 2 reps each, both sides. Reinforces depth precision and footwork on every route in the playbook.

name: Quickness Release Drill
desc: WR fires off the line of scrimmage as fast as possible with a maximum two-step release. First two steps must be violent enough to defeat press alignment before the route begins.

name: Fade-Stop Combination
desc: WR runs the fade to drive the corner deep, then a paired receiver runs a quick stop route in the area the corner vacated. QB reads the corner's depth and delivers to the stop if the corner sinks.

name: Backshoulder Fade Red Zone
desc: QB and WR rep the back-shoulder fade from the 10-yard line. WR presses the corner inside, the QB places the ball on the back shoulder to the pylon, and the WR adjusts without breaking stride.

name: H-Back Route Running
desc: H-backs or fullbacks run their specialized routes — arrow, angle, shoot, seam, and swing — with timing built in from pre-motion or alignment. Develops this hybrid position's pass-catching dimension.

name: Breaking Down on the Break
desc: WR works the gather steps — the two small adjustment steps before the final plant and cut — that separate elite route-runners from average ones. Develop the precise gather-to-break sequence on every route.

name: Concentration Catch Series
desc: Progressively harder concentration catches — ball arrives fast, arrives from unexpected angles, or the receiver must track it while a defender is in their face. Pure focus and hand concentration.

name: WR Speed Development
desc: WRs work 20-yard speed explosions off the line — full sprint at 100%. Develops the vertical speed needed to threaten deep coverage and get on top of corners. Timed weekly for accountability.

name: Out Route vs. Cover 2 Corner
desc: WR works the out route against a Cover 2 corner who is threatening to undercut it. WR must sell vertical with a vertical stem before breaking out. QB delivers before the corner can close.

name: WR Screen Route Execution
desc: WR runs a bubble or tunnel screen route — must stay behind the line of scrimmage, catch cleanly, and read the blocks in front. One missed block assignment and the play is a loss.

name: Tight End Red Zone Tree
desc: TEs run their specific red zone route tree — corner, flat, stop, and back-shoulder fade — from various alignments. The TE's size advantage makes them the primary red zone target in most offenses.

name: Pattern-Match vs. Man
desc: WRs run routes against man coverage and practice finding the soft spot in the defender's coverage — stem inside to draw the CB, break back outside for the completion.

name: In-Breaking Route Timing
desc: WR works all in-breaking routes — slant, dig, and cross — with the QB. Focus on making the break at the exact depth to create a rhythm throw from the QB's 3 or 5-step drop.

name: Run Block Technique for WR
desc: Comprehensive WR blocking session — stalk block on the corner, crack on the safety, and cutoff block on a linebacker. Every rep graded on initial contact and the ability to sustain the block.

name: Boundary WR vs. Press
desc: Boundary WR works one-on-one against press coverage — the boundary is a built-in disadvantage. Develop the inside release that creates separation on the compressed side of the field.

name: Wheel Route Timing
desc: RB or slot WR runs the wheel — releases flat, swings out, then bends up the sideline. QB must throw the ball before the receiver turns upfield. Timing is exact — too early is incomplete, too late is covered.

name: Competition Catch Circuit
desc: WR and CB compete on every catch in a circuit — back shoulder, sideline, end zone fade, and crossing route. Win-loss tracked. Final competition forces execution under direct defensive pressure.

name: Low-Pad Release Drill
desc: WR practices releasing off the line of scrimmage while staying below the CB's hands — dip under the jam, roll the shoulder, and get vertical. Develops the inside release used against physical corners.

name: 3-Step Route Connect
desc: WR and QB practice the 3-step-and-throw connection — slant, hitch, and quick out. QB's third step and the WR's break must occur simultaneously. No hesitation on either end.

name: Deep Comeback Route
desc: WR runs a 16-18 yard comeback — drives vertically at full speed, plants the outside foot, and snaps back to the QB. The vertical push must be real enough to make the corner run with the WR.

name: Receiving in Zone Soft Spot
desc: WR runs a route into a zone coverage soft spot — finds the window between defenders, settles, and presents the hands as a target. Develops the zone-beating route running used against soft coverage.

name: Slot WR Horizontal Stretch
desc: Slot WR works routes designed to stretch the defense horizontally — speed out, whip, and stick route. Forces the linebacker to chase the slot to the edge and opens the interior for other routes.

name: Tempo Route Running
desc: WRs run routes at no-huddle pace — 3 back-to-back routes in 30 seconds. Conditioning meets technique. Receivers must execute precise routes even when fatigued. Simulates late-game tempo offense.

name: TE Leverage vs. LB in Man
desc: TE works one-on-one against a linebacker in man coverage — physical release off the line, acceleration through the linebacker's grasp, and separation on the route. Develops the TE's matchup advantage.

name: Crack Block on Safety
desc: WR works the crack block on a safety playing run support — stem route to draw the safety's attention, then crack down on a hard shoulder block. Legal and effective perimeter blocking technique.

name: Receiver Film Study
desc: WR position group reviews their route-running film from the last game — identify releases that were getting jammed, routes run at wrong depth, and missed YAC opportunities. Coach-led correction session.

name: Four-Way Release Drill
desc: WR practices releasing four ways from the line of scrimmage — outside release, inside release, push release, and speed release. Each technique has a specific use case based on the defender's leverage.

name: Crosser Timing vs. Cover 3
desc: WR runs a crossing route at 8-10 yards against a Cover 3 hook-curl defender. Must find the soft spot in the zone, settle briefly, and be ready to absorb contact from the safety closing over the top.

name: WR Acceleration Drill
desc: WRs work three-step burst acceleration — short choppy steps followed by a powerful elongation into a full sprint. Develops the ability to go from route-running speed to full speed at the break point.

name: Spot Route Precision
desc: WR runs a spot route — 6-yard break to an exact spot on the field, sit down, and present as a target. The ball must arrive precisely as the WR arrives at the spot. No separation needed; positioning is everything.

name: Triangle Route Concept
desc: Three receivers create a triangle read for the QB — seam, out, and flat in a triangular spacing. QB reads the linebacker to determine which receiver gets the ball based on coverage rotation.

name: Catch Rate Accountability
desc: Each WR catches 20 balls from JUGS at various locations. Drop rate is tracked and posted. Competition creates accountability — players with the lowest drop rate lead the position group drill.

name: End Zone Back-Pylon Route
desc: WR works the back-pylon throw from the 15-yard line — vertical push, corner break to the back pylon, catch over the outside shoulder. The most common red zone route requires constant rehearsal.

name: Mismatch Route Exploitation
desc: Identify the team's best mismatch — TE on a linebacker, WR on a nickel DB — and run that receiver against the mismatched defender in live reps. Develop game-plan-specific competitive reps.

name: Quick Out with Block
desc: WR runs a quick out at 5 yards, catches the ball, and immediately blocks the corner on the run after catch. Combines receiving and blocking in a single rep — reflects real-game YAC blocking demands.

name: Seam Splitting Drill
desc: Two WRs run vertical seams — inside the hash and outside the numbers — forcing the safety to choose one. QB attacks the uncovered seam. Develops the spacing needed to split a two-high defense.

name: Check-Down Route Execution
desc: RBs and TEs work check-down routes — flat, angle, and pivot — that give the QB a safe outlet on a designed hot route. Develops the timing and positioning of the safety valve in the passing game.

name: WR vs. Press Zone Combo
desc: Corner plays press at the line but transitions to zone coverage after 5 yards. WR must recognize the transition and adjust the route accordingly — stem like man, find the soft spot like zone.

name: Two-Point Conversion Routes
desc: WRs run their two-point conversion route package — quick slant, back-shoulder fade, and designed option route. Defense plays live with no cushion. Every rep is high-stakes competition.

name: Route Depth Precision
desc: Cones are placed at exact route depths — 5, 8, 10, 12, 14 yards. WR must break at the cone each time without looking down. Develops internalized route depth precision through spatial memory.

name: Body Catch Correction Drill
desc: Any receiver who body-catches runs this correction drill — balls thrown to various angles and heights, hands only, no chest. 20 consecutive clean catches required before moving on.

name: High-Low Combination Routes
desc: Two receivers run high-low combination routes — one at 6 yards, one at 15 — creating a conflict for the underneath defender. QB reads the defender and attacks the vacated level.

name: Full Receiving Circuit
desc: Comprehensive receiving circuit — JUGS volume catching, one-on-one routes, contested catches in the end zone, and a YAC open-field station. Every receiver rotates through all four stations in the period.

name: Switch Route Coordination
desc: Two adjacent receivers cross pre-snap or at the snap to switch route assignments. The switch forces the defense to exchange coverage assignments — confusion created is the weapon.

name: Flat Route Catch and Turn
desc: RB or WR catches a flat route and immediately turns upfield. The catch, turn, and burst must happen in one fluid motion — hesitation eliminates the play's effectiveness.

name: Vertical Stack Release
desc: Two WRs in a vertical stack alignment — one in front of the other — release simultaneously on complementary routes. The outside receiver clears the corner; the inside receiver exploits the coverage gap.

name: Physical Red Zone Battles
desc: Full competitive receiving drills inside the 10 — WR vs. CB, TE vs. safety, all live. Track touchdowns vs. stops. Physical red zone battles are won by the team that practices them more aggressively.

name: Punt-Catch Under Pressure
desc: Returner catches high punts while defenders close from multiple directions at controlled speed. Develops the focus and mechanics needed to secure a punt under gunner pressure.

name: Situational Catch Drill
desc: Simulate specific game situations — 4th and 6 catch with a defender draped on the WR, 2-point conversion with zone pressure, sideline out with one foot in. Develops execution when it matters most.

name: WR Alignment vs. Alignment
desc: WRs practice aligning correctly based on every formation tag in the playbook — boundary, field, slot, nub, and bunch. Precise alignment is the non-negotiable first job of every wide receiver.

name: Full Receiving Period
desc: Comprehensive receiving position group period — walk-through routes, isolated JUGS work, 7-on-7 against live DBs, and red zone competitive catches. Every receiving position group represented.

name: Catch & Protect vs. Blitz
desc: Receiver catches a short route and immediately faces a blitzing defender closing hard. Must secure the ball, lower the shoulder, and either absorb the hit or make the defender miss. Builds toughness.

name: WR Competitive One-on-One
desc: All WRs compete one-on-one against all CBs in a full competitive period — win-loss tracked, results posted. Builds competitive accountability and reveals which matchups favor the offense on game day.

name: Zone Combo to Linebacker
desc: Guard and tackle work a playside zone combo — double-team the defensive tackle, then one releases to the linebacker. The release timing is critical — don't leave early or the DT blows up the run.

name: Pass Set Footwork
desc: Linemen work the kick-slide pass set in isolation — first step back and away from the defender, maintain inside leverage, and stay square. A perfect pass set is the foundation of all pass protection.

name: Redirect vs. Speed Rush
desc: O-lineman allows the rusher to get full speed, then redirects the rusher upfield using a push-pull technique. Develops the ability to use the rusher's momentum against them when beaten on the initial set.

name: Inside Zone Footwork
desc: OL works inside zone footwork without a snap — playside step, zone reach angle, and backside cutoff path. All five work in unison. Film the footwork sequence to check lateral coordination.

name: Reach vs. 3-Technique
desc: Guard works a reach block on a 3-technique defensive tackle — the hardest reach block assignment. First step must be flat and fast, winning leverage outside the DT's frame before he can fight.

name: Power Run Blocking
desc: Playside OL fires out on a power run — down block, kick-out, and log block assignments practiced in sequence with live defensive alignment. Develop the physical aggression needed in gap-scheme football.

name: Pass Pro vs. Inside Move
desc: OL works pass protection against a defender who attacks inside — works the redirect, resets the hands inside the defender's frame, and drives them upfield away from the QB.

name: Slide Protection Calls
desc: Center makes the Mike call and identifies the slide direction — protection slides to one side with the back responsible for the backside blitzer. Full OL and RB communication before and at the snap.

name: Down Block Technique
desc: Linemen work down blocks on inside defenders — step at a 45-degree angle, get the inside hand on the defender's hip, and drive them away from the gap being targeted by the run.

name: Counter Pull Path
desc: Guards work the counter pull — pivot open, flat pull path to the opposite gap, kick out the defensive end or log block the linebacker. The pull path angle determines the success of the kick-out.

name: Run and Pass Read
desc: OL reads a defender's alignment and decides whether to fire out on a run block or set for pass protection. Works against a defensive player who randomly changes pre-snap to run or pass technique.

name: Hat-on-Hat Run Drill
desc: Every OL is assigned a specific defender. On the snap, each lineman must get their hat on their assigned man and drive. Simple hat-on-hat blocking develops the physical winning mentality of a run-first OL.

name: Pancake Block Circuit
desc: Linemen work the finishing block — maintain contact through the defender's fall, ride them to the ground, and stand over them. Builds the physical dominance mentality through competitive contact.

name: Trap Pull Technique
desc: Guard works the trap pull — short pivot open step, tight flat path down the line, and a kick-out on the penetrating defender. The trap works only when the guard gets to the trap hole before the DL.

name: Turnback Protection
desc: Backside linemen work turnback protection — cut off pursuing backside defenders on outside run plays. Low pad level, cut-block technique, or reach-and-seal depending on alignment.

name: Anchor vs. Bull Rush
desc: OL works anchoring against a bull-rush attack — sink the hips, widen the base, and redirect the rush upfield. The anchor is a physical contest of leverage and lower-body strength.

name: Wham Block Technique
desc: H-back or TE works the wham block — comes from behind the OL to hit a down defensive lineman who has penetrated inside. Timing of the wham requires coordination with the guard's release.

name: Five-Man Pass Pro vs. 4-3
desc: Full OL runs five-man pass protection against a live 4-3 defense. No RB help — line must communicate protections and handle all four rushers plus any fifth rusher from the linebacker level.

name: Gap Exchange vs. Stunt
desc: OL works gap exchanges against T-T and E-T stunts — one lineman passes off their rusher to a partner who picks up the looping stunt. Communication and quick hands are the key to defeating any stunt.

name: Center Snap Consistency
desc: Center works snaps to the QB — both under-center and shotgun — focusing on snap accuracy and consistent hand position. Work snaps to both sides of the QB's body and at varying depths.

name: OL Footwork Ladder
desc: Linemen run the agility ladder with emphasis on lateral quickness, short step cadence, and maintaining a low athletic position through every pattern. Foot speed for a big man is a competitive advantage.

name: Long Trap Guard Pull
desc: Guard pulls across the center for a long trap — the pull path is longer and the arrival angle is sharper. Develop the foot speed and flat path needed to reach the trap target before the run arrives.

name: Full Zone Blocking Period
desc: All five OL run inside and outside zone against a full defensive front for an extended period. Emphasis on combo timing, hand placement, and second-level release. Film every series for review.

name: Pass Protection vs. Blitz
desc: OL and RB protect against various blitz packages — overload left, overload right, A-gap pressure, and DB blitz. Identify the free rusher and adjust the protection call at the line of scrimmage.

name: Hand Re-Set Drill
desc: Linemen work resetting their hands after a failed first strike — punch, lose the hands, immediately reset inside, and punch again. Develops the quick-hands reaction needed when the initial punch misses.

name: First Step Quickness
desc: All five linemen fire out on snap count for a single isolated first step — timed with a stopwatch. Shortest time from snap to first step wins. Develops the get-off quickness that every run needs.

name: Backside Cut-Off Block
desc: Backside linemen work the cut-off block on a defender pursuing the run from the backside — attack the defender's outside number, turn the hips, and prevent the cutback lane from being closed.

name: Zone Read Block Scheme
desc: OL runs zone read blocking — all five block their zone assignments while the end is left unblocked for the QB's read. The blocking scheme must be clean regardless of the QB's decision.

name: Inside Release vs. 5-Tech
desc: Tackle works releasing inside on a 5-technique defensive end — necessary when the end is covered on inside zone or trap plays. Drive the end upfield, then release to the second level.

name: OL Communication Pre-Snap
desc: OL goes through full pre-snap communication — Mike ID, strength call, protection slide, hot route recognition, and snap count confirmation — against multiple defensive alignments shown by a scout.

name: Stretch Block vs. Angle
desc: Linemen work the stretch block against a defender aligned at an angle — the hardest reach block scenario. First step must beat the defender to the spot outside their frame before they can close.

name: Post-Snap Identification
desc: OL practices identifying defensive stunts and blitzes post-snap and immediately communicating the adjustment. Quick verbal ID and smooth hand-off of rushing assignments builds protection IQ.

name: Drive Block Sled Work
desc: Linemen drive the 7-man sled for 10 consecutive yards — maintaining pad level, driving the feet, and pushing through contact. No leaning into the sled; drive it with leg power.

name: Sculling Technique for Guards
desc: Guards work the sculling technique — short, rapid lateral shuffle steps behind the line of scrimmage — used in pulling assignments to maintain low pad level while moving at speed.

name: Inside Hand Punch
desc: Linemen practice getting their hands inside the defender's chest — thumbs up, elbows in, inside their shoulder pads. A single drill focused only on hand placement quality across 20 reps.

name: Gap Blocking All Schemes
desc: OL runs through all gap-scheme plays in sequence — power, counter, trap, and sweep — with the correct blocking assignment for each scheme. Film to identify which scheme needs the most work.

name: Max Protection Drill
desc: All five OL plus RB and TE in full max protection, holding the pocket for 4.5 seconds against a seven-man rush. Final depth plays require a clean pocket for a long time.

name: Chute Work for Pad Level
desc: Linemen fire through a blocking chute at various widths — forces knee bend and a low pad level on every rep. Anyone whose pads touch the chute repeats the rep immediately.

name: OL Unit Cohesion Period
desc: All five OL work in unison against a full defensive front — run blocks, pass sets, and stunt pickups in a continuous period. Developing the five-man cohesion that transforms good individual linemen into a great unit.

name: Competitive Run Block 1-on-1
desc: Each OL competes one-on-one against a DL in a blocked run rep — blocker wins by driving the defender 3 yards, defender wins by shedding and making the tackle. Win-loss tracked and posted.

name: Reach Block vs. Wide Technique
desc: OL works a reach block against a defender playing a wide alignment — tackles on 5-techniques, guards on 3-techniques. The wide alignment makes every reach block a step-for-step footrace.

name: Pulling for Crack-Replace
desc: Guard pulls outside when a WR cracks inside — the WR takes the safety, the guard fills for the WR and blocks the corner. Crack-replace blocking is a key component of perimeter run success.

name: Free Release vs. Second Level
desc: Linemen work releasing off the line and blocking a linebacker or safety at the second level — get a hat on the linebacker before he can make the tackle. Downfield blocking wins long runs.

name: Silent Snap Count Drill
desc: OL practices snapping and firing out on a silent snap count — visual signal from the QB only. Develops the ability to execute in a crowd noise situation where verbal cadence cannot be heard.

name: Cross-Block Technique
desc: Guard and tackle cross-block each other's assignments — guard down, tackle wraps outside. Timing of the cross-block is exact — one fires first, the second fills immediately behind.

name: Full Pass Pro Evaluation
desc: Full-team pass protection evaluation against an entire defensive front plus a blitz. Coach grades every rep. Both the individual technique and the collective unit protection are evaluated.

name: Split-Zone TE Coordination
desc: TE coordinates the split-zone kick-out block with the OL's inside zone scheme. OL runs inside zone away from the TE; TE kicks out the backside end. Timing of the TE's entry is critical.

name: Heavy Formation Alignment
desc: OL practices aligning in heavy formations — jumbo, full-house, two TEs. Correct splits, communication of the new center, and pre-snap calls adjusted for heavy personnel. Alignment sets the scheme.

name: Quick Game Pass Protection
desc: OL works 3-step pass protection — quick initial set, absorb the rush, and the QB gets the ball out in under 1.8 seconds. Quick game protection is different from 7-step — urgency over depth.

name: Run Block Film Review
desc: Full OL watches film of their run-blocking performance together. Every combo, reach, and pull is evaluated. Coach makes individual corrections; unit identifies the play where communication broke down.

name: Second-Level Block Progression
desc: Linemen work releasing to the second level — identify the linebacker, take the correct pursuit angle, and arrive with full speed before the linebacker can set his feet.

name: Tug and Reset Drill
desc: Partner tugs both arms of a lineman — they must reset their hands immediately and punch back inside. Develops the recovery reflex needed when a defender tries to disengage or counter the punch.

name: Individual Position Period
desc: Each OL gets individual technique reps with a position coach — center works snaps and double-teams, guards work pulls and combo blocks, tackles work pass sets and edge blocking.

name: Against Zone Blitz
desc: OL and RB work protections against zone blitz packages — defenders dropping off and others coming late. The changing coverage responsibility mid-play forces faster communication adjustments.

name: Full Run Install Period
desc: Dedicated OL run install period — all new run plays installed with the full OL at walk speed, then full speed. Every lineman must know their assignment on every play before practice ends.

name: Kick-Slide Lateral Speed
desc: Tackles work kick-slide lateral movement at maximum speed — the longer the kick-slide can be sustained without giving ground, the more likely they are to pass-protect the edge rusher.

name: Post-Snap Count Adjustment
desc: OL practices adjusting their post-snap assignments when a defender stunts or twists. React to the new rusher without hesitation — hand-off and pickup must be seamless.

name: Six-Man Protection
desc: Five OL plus a tight end work six-man protection — extra blocker used on a specific side to guarantee the edge on a 7-step drop or a bootleg. TE learns their protection assignment within the scheme.

name: Stalemate to Victory
desc: Linemen and defenders lock up in a stalemate — equal leverage — then compete to break the tie through lower body drive, hand re-set, and footwork. Teaches winning from a neutral position.

name: Team Run Period Evaluation
desc: Full-team run evaluation period — 15 plays from the run game against live defense. OL graded on every play. Film reviewed same day. Both individual technique and unit cohesion evaluated side by side.

name: Zone Blocking Aiming Points
desc: Linemen work their aiming points on zone blocks — the near number of the adjacent defender, the near hip on a playside reach, and the far number on a backside cutoff. Precision aiming points make zone runs work.

name: One-on-One Pass Rush Circuit
desc: Every OL takes 5 one-on-one pass protection reps against every DL. Win-loss tracked. Circuit format ensures maximum reps and reveals every individual mismatch before game day.

name: Trench Conditioning Sprint
desc: OL finishes practice with 5-yard explosion sprints in pairs — short distance, maximum effort. 10 reps with 30 seconds rest. Develops the short-burst conditioning needed for a full game at the line of scrimmage.

name: Snap to Block Timing
desc: Center snaps and immediately fires out into a run block while the rest of the OL fires simultaneously. Develop the synchronized snap-to-block timing that makes the OL a cohesive explosive unit.

name: OL Flex and Mobility Circuit
desc: OL-specific mobility work — hip flexor stretches, thoracic rotations, and ankle mobility. Large linemen need joint mobility work specific to the positions they play at high intensity every day.

name: Play Fake Sell for OL
desc: OL practices executing a run-block action on a play-action pass — fire out like run, then redirect to pass protection. The run fake is only effective if the linemen sell it convincingly.

name: Guard-Center Combo
desc: Guard and center work their specific combo block pairings — nose/1-tech double team, 3-tech trap, and A-gap zone combo. Two-man cohesion developed in isolation before adding the full five.

name: Tackle-Guard Combo
desc: Tackle and guard work their combo block pairings on the edge — 4-3 over front, 5-tech, and 3-4 end alignment. Specific combo assignments developed for each defensive front they will face.

name: Offensive Line Film Breakdown
desc: Full OL watches film of the upcoming opponent's defensive line — tendencies, favorite stunts, pass rush moves, and run fits. Each lineman identifies specific techniques they will need to counter.

name: Full OL Competition Period
desc: Full offensive line competes in run and pass reps with win-loss tracked for every snap. Competitive pressure exposes the weakest links and reinforces the physical aggression needed for game-ready performance.

name: Ghost Rush Technique
desc: D-lineman works against air — no blocker — visualizing the block, reacting to it, and executing a full pass rush move. Develops muscle memory for the move sequence before live contact reps.

name: Under & Over Move Series
desc: D-lineman works the full under-and-over move sequence — set up the under, execute the over-the-top swim, convert to the QB. Practiced as a complete sequence against a live blocker at half speed.

name: Two-Gap Technique
desc: Defensive tackle works two-gap responsibility — engage the offensive lineman, control both adjacent gaps, and hold the point of attack without getting moved. Requires superior base and hand strength.

name: One-Gap Penetration
desc: D-lineman fires into a single assigned gap at maximum speed — penetrate past the line of scrimmage and disrupt the backfield. Low pad level, violent hands, and no hesitation off the line.

name: Chop Block Recognition
desc: D-lineman works recognizing a cut block threat — feel the cut coming, stack the OL with the hands, and hop over or sidestep the cut. Develops the self-protection instinct needed against zone-cut teams.

name: Long Stick Reach
desc: D-lineman works extending the arms fully to deliver a long power punch to the blocker's chest — extending the reach point before the blocker can get inside. A longer strike controls the engagement.

name: Dip & Bend Edge Rush
desc: Edge rusher works the dip-and-bend move around the corner — lower the pad level, bend at the waist without falling, and flatten to the QB. The bend must be under control without loss of speed.

name: Swim Over the Top
desc: D-lineman knocks down the blocker's arm and swims the opposite arm over the top to gain inside position. Practiced in isolation 20 times before working against a live blocker.

name: 3-Technique Pass Rush
desc: Interior 3-technique DT works a dedicated pass rush plan — initial punch to engage the guard, first move to the shoulder, counter inside. Interior pass rush is the most direct path to the QB.

name: DL Stunt Communication
desc: D-line works T-T and E-T stunt communication — call the stunt, confirm the assignment, and execute with exact timing so neither player is out of position when the stunt is countered by the OL.

name: Run Pursuit from Backside
desc: Backside D-linemen work pursuit of a run going away from them — shed the block, cross the face, and pursue at the correct angle to cut off the ball carrier or force the cut back.

name: Stack & Shed Technique
desc: DL works stacking the blocker's punch — hands inside the OL's shoulders — then violently shedding to either side. Stack first, shed second. Defenders who try to shed before stacking get driven off the ball.

name: Flatten and Finish
desc: Edge rusher turns the corner and practices the flatten move — redirect from an outside rush to a direct path to the QB's set point. Develop the ability to get there before the pocket collapses.

name: 1-on-1 Pass Rush Competition
desc: DL vs. OL in live one-on-one pass rush reps. Win for the DL means a touch on the QB target. Win for the OL means a clean pocket for 3 seconds. Win-loss tracked and posted after each session.

name: Bear Crawl Power Builder
desc: D-linemen bear crawl 10 yards at maximum intensity. Builds shoulder stability, core strength, and the hip-drive mechanics that transfer directly to low-pad-level run defense and pass rush power.

name: Run Fit vs. Spread Formation
desc: DL adjusts run fits against a spread formation with only 5 or 6 offensive linemen. Less mass at the line means faster read responsibilities — DL must read and react quicker than against base formations.

name: End Contain Drill
desc: Defensive ends work maintaining outside contain — squeeze the C-gap, close toward the QB on a keeper, and never over-pursue inside. Contain is the first job of the end on every play.

name: Counter to the Inside
desc: After setting up an outside rush, D-lineman counters hard inside with a club-and-rip move. The inside counter is the most effective counter to a tackle who is overcommitting to the outside rush.

name: Hand Fight vs. Jab Step
desc: D-lineman reacts to an OL jab step — a short directional step that baits a wrong reaction. Work staying disciplined with the hands, not over-reacting to the jab, and maintaining a neutral position.

name: Low-and-Tight Bull Rush
desc: DT works a low bull rush specifically — sink the hips lower than the OL, get the hands under the shoulder pads, and drive upfield with the legs churning. Low bull rush creates instant backward movement.

name: Slant Through the Gap
desc: DL works a gap slant on the snap — fire at a 45-degree angle through the assigned gap before the OL can redirect. A decisive violent slant disrupts both runs and passes by penetrating the line.

name: Tackle vs. Cut Block
desc: DL works defeating a zone cut block — punch down on the blocker's head or shoulders before they get to the legs. React early and punch to prevent the cut. Leg protection technique practiced 20 reps.

name: Three-Point Stance Explosion
desc: DL fires out of a three-point stance on the snap — maximum first-step quickness, low pad level, and immediate pressure on the blocker. Measured by the time from snap to contact.

name: Pressure Pocket Collapse
desc: Interior DL coordinates a pocket collapse with edge rushers — inside rushers push the pocket up while edge rushers bend the corner. Full coordination creates a collapsing pocket that forces a quick throw.

name: Spin Inside Counter
desc: After engaging the blocker outside, DL executes a spin move to the inside. The spin must be sharp and low — full 360 degrees in under half a second. Practiced to both sides 15 times before live reps.

name: Leverage & Anchor Work
desc: DL works maintaining leverage under a blocker's pressure — resisting being driven off the ball while maintaining a wide base and flat back. Strength combined with technique is the anchor formula.

name: DL Film Breakdown
desc: DL watches film of the upcoming opponent's offensive line — identify their zone blocking tendencies, who pulls, and which linemen leave early on combo blocks. Preparation beats athleticism.

name: Zone Blitz Drop for DL
desc: One DL drops into a shallow zone on a zone blitz. DL works a quick sprint to the coverage area, turns to find the QB, and breaks on the ball. Develops an unexpected coverage dimension from a DL.

name: Jet Stunts vs. OL
desc: DL runs jet stunts — E-E and T-T games where two linemen cross each other's gaps simultaneously. Both men must get off the line clean and cross without collision.

name: Shed and Tackle in Space
desc: DL works shedding a blocker then making the tackle on a ball carrier in space — two-step sequence at game speed. The shed must be violent and the tackle approach immediate.

name: Pass Rush Moves Inventory
desc: Each DL performs every pass rush move in their repertoire — rip, swim, spin, club-rip, ghost, inside counter — against a live blocker at three-quarter speed. 20 reps of each in a full inventory period.

name: TE Chip Block Recognition
desc: Edge rusher works recognizing and defeating a TE chip block — absorb the chip, stay on course to the QB, and don't lose the rush lane when the chip arrives. Short violent contact, then reset and rush.

name: First Step Lateral Explosion
desc: DL fires on the snap with a lateral first step to the slant direction — maximum lateral speed off the ball. A fast lateral first step beats any blocker trying to adjust their footwork to the slant.

name: Tackle Fundamentals
desc: DL works open-field tackle mechanics — approach angle, breakdown, hit through the ball carrier's chest, and wrap both arms. Missed tackles from DL after a blocked pass are high-cost plays.

name: Two-Man Game vs. Double Team
desc: Two D-linemen coordinate to beat a double-team block — one draws the double team while the other works free into the backfield. Timing and communication of the two-man stunt is the key.

name: Hump Move Technique
desc: Interior DL works the hump move — a short quick inside fake followed by an outside rip. Baits the guard's counter and then attacks the hump gap with the rip before the guard can react.

name: Stunts vs. Zone Protection
desc: DL runs stunts against a zone-blocking OL — T-T and E-T games that attack the zone's hand-off assignments. Zone protection's weakness is the looping DL; exploit it with coordinated stunts.

name: Full DL Period vs. OL
desc: Full defensive line works in a structured period against the full offensive line — run fits, pass rush, and stunts across multiple fronts and movements. Film graded and individual corrections made after.

name: Read and React vs. Run
desc: DL reads the offensive line's first step on the snap and reacts to run or pass — if the OL fires out, attack the run; if they set pass, implement the pass rush plan. Develop post-snap key reading.

name: Three-Down Package Install
desc: Install the three-down defensive line package — 3-4 base, nickel, and dime fronts. Each DL learns their alignment and assignment in each package. Full install against a scout offense.

name: Explosive First Move Drill
desc: DL works the first move of their pass rush plan against air — maximum speed and explosion on the first move only. No second or third move; focus entirely on making the first move perfect.

name: Bend-and-Flatten Around Edge
desc: Edge rusher bends around the tackle's outside edge and flattens toward the QB's set position. The bend angle must be tight to the tackle — taking a wide arc wastes time and allows the QB to step up.

name: Gap Discipline vs. Misdirection
desc: DL maintains their gap assignment against counter, reverse, and misdirection plays. The most disciplined lineman doesn't over-pursue and leaves a gap for the cut-back run.

name: Defensive Tackle vs. Center
desc: DT works specifically against a center's snap-and-fire technique — the DT must beat the center's first step to gain an immediate advantage at the snap. Develops the interior penetration game.

name: DL Conditioning Sprint
desc: DL runs 5-yard explosion sprints in sets of 10 with 30-second rest intervals. Short burst conditioning for the line of scrimmage — every play is a 3-5 yard explosion followed by a rest.

name: Edge Rush vs. Tight End
desc: Edge rusher works against a TE or H-back in pass protection — different from a tackle, TEs are shorter and use different technique. Adjust the rush plan to beat a TE's chip-and-block style.

name: 4-3 Under vs. 4-3 Over
desc: DL works both 4-3 Under and Over alignments — different gap responsibilities for each front. Walk through the alignment and assignment adjustments, then execute at full speed in live reps.

name: Long Arms Technique
desc: DL with long arms works maximizing their reach advantage — punch at the maximum extension point before the OL can get inside. Long arms must strike first to control the engagement.

name: Finishing the Sack
desc: Once the pass rusher beats the blocker, they work the final step to the QB — wrap up, strip the ball, or force an intentional grounding. The finish matters as much as beating the block.

name: Full Rush Lane Discipline
desc: All four DL work their pass rush lanes — each stays in their lane and takes their assigned path to the QB. Uncoordinated rushers allow the QB to step up; disciplined lanes collapse the whole pocket.

name: Nose Tackle Two-Gap
desc: Nose tackle works two-gap technique specifically — head-up on the center, control the A-gap on both sides, and hold the point of attack without movement. A dominant nose tackle changes the run game.

name: End-Tackle Game Timing
desc: Defensive end and tackle coordinate an E-T game — end goes first inside, tackle loops outside behind the end's rush. The timing must be precise so the OL can't pass off the stunt cleanly.

name: Coordinated Pocket Pressure
desc: Full DL coordinates their rush plan as a unit — interior pressure forces the QB to step, edge rushers flatten from the outside. Synchronized pressure from all four rushers is harder to protect against.

name: DL Competition Period
desc: Full DL vs. OL competition period — run fits and pass rush alternated for 20 plays with win-loss tracked. Scoreboard creates game-like intensity and exposes the players who perform under pressure.

name: Tackle-Tackle Stunt
desc: Two defensive tackles coordinate a T-T stunt — one penetrates inside while the other loops outside behind. The guard and center must choose; one DT will get a free rush into the backfield.

name: Counter to Swim
desc: DL sets up an inside counter rush, then counters back outside with a swim move. Double-counter pass rush plan — each move feeds the next, keeping the blocker guessing until the DL is past them.

name: DL Inside Leverage
desc: DL works maintaining inside leverage throughout a pass rush — keeping the shoulder inside the blocker's frame and the rush path tight to the QB's set point. Inside leverage shortens the path to the QB.

name: Run Stop Evaluation
desc: DL is evaluated on their run-stop ability across 10 run plays — gap assignment, pad level, and shedding to make the tackle. Defensive coordinator grades each rep and identifies the weakest run stopper.

name: DL Alignment vs. Every Front
desc: DL works alignment in every defensive front they use — 4-3 Over, 4-3 Under, 3-4, Bear, and Nickel. Correct alignment is the first job; assignment only works from the right alignment.

name: Full Defensive Line Period
desc: Comprehensive defensive line period — run fits, one-on-one pass rush, stunts, and coordinated pressure in a full position group workout. Every DL gets a minimum of 15 high-quality reps.

name: Pass Rush Setup Drill
desc: DL works the setup phase of the pass rush — the initial contact, the tempo change, and the load before the first move. Setting up the blocker correctly makes every pass rush move more effective.

name: Gap Shooting vs. A-Gap
desc: A-gap players work gap shooting on the snap — maximum penetration into the A-gap before the center or guard can stop them. Gap shooting creates immediate backfield chaos.

name: Rushing the Passer Evaluation
desc: Full pass rush evaluation — each DL gets 5 live one-on-one reps against an OL. Coach rates each rep: quick pressure, late pressure, or no pressure. Data drives individual pass rush development plans.

name: Nose in 3-4 Alignment
desc: Nose tackle works alignment and technique in a 3-4 base — head-up on the center with wider base than in a 4-man front. Two-gap responsibility on both A-gaps while eating the double-team.

name: DL Hustle Plays
desc: DL works hustle plays — chasing the ball carrier from behind, pursuing a screen in the flat, and running down a scrambling QB. Hustle plays from DL change games and define championship-level effort.

name: Contain vs. QB Scramble
desc: Edge rushers practice containing a scrambling QB — rush hard, convert to containment when the QB escapes, and keep the QB's run inside the pursuit. Don't over-pursue and let the QB outside.

name: Explosion Off the Ball
desc: DL works first-step explosion off a visual snap signal — reactive first step that beats any OL's kick-slide before they can complete it. The DL who gets off fastest wins the line of scrimmage.

name: Technique vs. Zone Cut
desc: DL works specifically against zone-blocking cut techniques — read the cut coming, stack with the hands, and skip over or shed to the playside. Zone teams cut-block; DL must be prepared.

name: Full Defense DL Insert
desc: DL works within the full 11-man defense during a team period — run fits coordinated with linebackers, pass rush lanes kept in context of the coverage behind them. Total defensive coordination.

name: DL Closing Speed to Ball
desc: DL practices closing speed to the ball carrier after shedding the initial block — immediate burst from the block shed to the tackle point. The time between shed and tackle determines if the run gains extra yards.

name: Pass Rush from LB
desc: LBs work blitz pass rush moves — DB-style speed rush around the edge, inside bull rush, and a counter spin. Linebackers who can win one-on-one on a blitz are a major pressure multiplier.

name: Hook-Curl Zone Drop
desc: LBs drop into hook-curl zones, expanding with receivers running to the flat and sitting down against receivers stopping in the zone. Develops the zone-reading patience needed in underneath coverage.

name: Lateral Run Fit
desc: LBs work fitting outside zone runs — take the correct lateral path, stay clean of blocks, and fit the runner inside-out. Pursuit angle on outside zone must be flat or slightly downhill to cut off the run.

name: Reverse & Counter Read
desc: LBs practice staying disciplined against reverses and counter plays — not over-pursuing on the initial flow, maintaining gap integrity, and reacting to the misdirection without abandoning their gap.

name: Drop in Man vs. RB
desc: LB works man coverage on an RB — chip the RB at the line of scrimmage to disrupt the release, then turn and run. Develop the hip-sink to sprint transition needed to match a fast back in man coverage.

name: Linebacker vs. Screen
desc: LB diagnoses a screen and pursues to the flat — read the lineman releasing, feel the QB setting up too deep, and redirect immediately to the flat before the screen develops. First to the flat wins.

name: Shed and Tackle Drill
desc: LB works shedding a blocker and making the tackle — hands on the blocker's chest, violent shed to the ball side, and immediate tackle break-down. Can't tackle a runner while engaged with a blocker.

name: ILB A-Gap Blitz
desc: Inside LB works an A-gap blitz — time the snap, hit the gap low and hard, and get to the QB before the center can adjust. The A-gap blitz is one of the most explosive plays in any defense.

name: Open-Field Tackle
desc: LBs work open-field tackles — approach the ball carrier in space, break down at 2-3 yards, and drive through the near hip with both arms wrapping. Missed tackles from LBs are big-play opportunities.

name: Curl-Flat Drop
desc: Outside LB drops to the curl-flat zone — expand to the flat on a flat route, stay in the curl against a deep out or curl route, and never let a receiver split the curl and flat at the same time.

name: Zone Blitz Drop Assignment
desc: LB drops into coverage on a zone blitz while a DL or DB rushes in their place. LB must get to the coverage area quickly enough to replace the rushing defender without exposing a seam.

name: Playing the Run From a 3-4
desc: Outside LBs work their run assignments in a 3-4 base — set the edge, slow-play the blocker, and force the run inside or bounce it out depending on their assignment. Edge LBs set the tone for the run game.

name: Blitz vs. Screen Recognition
desc: LB reads screen indicators while rushing a blitz — if linemen release on the blitz, convert from blitz to pursuit immediately. Converting mid-play from blitz to screen coverage is an elite LB skill.

name: Weak-Side LB Assignments
desc: Weakside LB works their full run and pass assignments — weakside gap responsibility, zone drop to the weakside, and blitz path from the weak side. Develop the complete weakside LB skill set.

name: Stack Behind DL
desc: ILB works stacking directly behind a DL — reading through the DL's block to identify the run gap before moving. Patience behind the line and then decisive movement when the gap is revealed.

name: Mike LB Leadership Calls
desc: Mike LB works making pre-snap calls — identify the Mike alignment for the offense, call the front adjustment, communicate coverage rotation, and confirm the blitz package. IQ and communication drilled.

name: Sam LB in 3-4 Hybrid
desc: Sam LB works their hybrid role in a 3-4 — set the edge on run, drop into coverage on pass, rush on specific blitz calls. Versatility is the Sam LB's value; practice every dimension of the role.

name: Cover 2 Flat Drop
desc: In Cover 2, the LB takes the flat zone responsibility — expand quickly to the flat, take away the quick throw to the flat, and don't let the RB catch a check-down behind the line.

name: Matching Routes in Zone
desc: LBs work pattern-matching in zone — when a receiver enters their zone, stay with them until another defender can pick them up. Pattern-matching bridges man and zone coverage responsibilities.

name: Linebacker Film Study
desc: LBs review film of the opponent's favorite run schemes and pass routes — identify which backs they will face in coverage, which runs attack their gap, and what blitzes will work against their protection.

name: Linebacker vs. TE One-on-One
desc: LB covers a TE one-on-one in isolation — physical press at the line, then run with the TE on a seam, corner, or drag route. Develop the athleticism and technique to neutralize the TE matchup.

name: Reading the Pulling Guard
desc: LB reads the pull direction of the offensive guard — if the guard pulls to the play side, follow the guard downhill; if he pulls backside, it's likely a counter or trap. Read, react, and fit correctly.

name: Overload Blitz Package
desc: Install an overload blitz — more rushers than the offense can block on one side. LBs must coordinate timing, alignment disguise, and rush lanes so the offense can't identify the free rusher pre-snap.

name: Red Dog Blitz
desc: LB executes a red dog blitz — drop back initially, then break through the line on a delayed count. The delayed blitz freezes the protection longer than an immediate rush and often arrives untouched.

name: Walk-Up Blitz Disguise
desc: LB walks up to the line pre-snap to disguise the blitz, then drops at the snap. Coach works timing the walk-up late enough to prevent QB adjustment but early enough to be in blitz alignment.

name: Combo Coverage Responsibilities
desc: LBs work combo coverage responsibilities — taking the first receiver to their side who enters the zone, matching through, and communicating hand-offs to the safety or corner behind them.

name: Pursuit to the Flat
desc: LBs pursue a ball carrier running a sweep or screen to the flat at maximum speed. Correct pursuit angle is critical — not over-running the play and not under-pursuing from the backside.

name: Run Game Technique Review
desc: LB position group reviews their run-defense technique on film — gap integrity, block-shedding timing, and tackle finish. Coach identifies the most common missed assignment from the last game.

name: 4-3 vs. 3-4 Alignment Switch
desc: LBs work their alignment and assignment in both 4-3 and 3-4 fronts. Different alignments change every gap responsibility; LBs must be fluent in both so the defense can show multiple fronts on game day.

name: LB Coverage Techniques
desc: LBs work their coverage technique menu — chip-and-cover on TEs, bail technique on vertical routes, and read-and-match on option routes. Coverage range makes a LB difficult to scheme around.

name: Blitz versus Empty Formation
desc: LBs work blitzing against an empty backfield — no RB to pick up the blitz. LBs have a cleaner path but must avoid the running QB and identify the hot route quickly.

name: Linebacker Conditioning
desc: LB-specific conditioning — lateral shuffles, 10-yard sprint-and-return, and angle tackle breaks in a circuit. LBs run the most total distance of any position; conditioning is their most important tool.

name: Second-Level Fit vs. Zone
desc: LBs work their second-level fit against zone blocking — OL will try to reach and release to them. LBs must avoid the block, find the ball, and fit the correct gap before the runner can cut back.

name: Pistol Formation Run Defense
desc: LBs adjust their run reads against pistol formation — RB directly behind a shotgun QB. The pistol blurs the run-pass tell; LBs must read the OL rather than the backfield for run-pass keys.

name: QB Contain in Zone Read
desc: Outside LB contains the QB on zone-read plays — don't bite on the give, maintain outside leverage on the QB pull, and force the QB to cut back into the pursuit.

name: Nickel LB in Space
desc: Nickel linebacker — or linebacker playing in a nickel front — works in space against slot receivers and RBs. Zone drops, man coverage, and blitz from the nickel alignment all practiced in rotation.

name: One-on-One versus RB Route
desc: ILB works one-on-one against an RB running a variety of routes — flat, angle, wheel, check-down. Most offenses target this matchup; the LB must be able to cover it competitively.

name: LB Stunt with DL
desc: LB coordinates a stunt with a DL — DL slants inside, LB times the blitz to run the gap behind the DL's slant. The LB's timing off the snap makes or breaks the stunt's success.

name: Zone Coverage Rotation
desc: LBs work coverage rotation calls — when the defense rotates from Cover 2 to Cover 3 or from a two-high to one-high look, LBs adjust their drop zone and depth accordingly.

name: Linebacker vs. Inside Run
desc: LBs work their fit vs. inside zone — read the OL release, stack the blocker, shed to the ballside gap, and make the tackle. Inside zone attacks LBs directly; gap sound technique is non-negotiable.

name: Disguise Coverage Drill
desc: LBs practice disguising their coverage — show a blitz look pre-snap, drop at the snap. Or walk up as if covering a receiver, then blitz at the snap. The element of surprise comes from disciplined disguise.

name: Linebacker Leverage Drill
desc: LBs work maintaining leverage on a blocker — stay low, keep the outside arm free, and work the blocker's inside shoulder to stay in the running lane. Leverage determines who controls the gap.

name: Weak-Side Contain
desc: Weak-side LB works contain responsibility — stay outside the WDE, don't cross the line of scrimmage without the ball committing to their side, and be the last line of defense on a weakside run.

name: Hands Technique vs. OL
desc: LBs work hand-fighting technique against downfield blocks from OL — punch first, lock out the blocker's arms, and disengage with a rip or swim before making the tackle.

name: Pattern-Match Coverage Drill
desc: LBs work pattern-matching — take responsibility for a route concept entering their area and stay with it until the play is over. Pattern-matching adds man-coverage precision to zone alignments.

name: Fumble Recovery Drill
desc: LBs work recovering fumbles — fall on the ball with full body wrap, protect it from defenders swiping at it, and secure possession. Fumble recoveries from LBs often come in the pile of a run play.

name: Force vs. Bounce
desc: LBs read whether to force the ball inside or let it bounce outside — based on outside help from the CB. If the corner is in position, force inside; if the corner is out of position, force outside.

name: Angle Tackling vs. Ball Carrier
desc: LBs practice angle tackling from all directions — head-on, from the side, and from behind. Correct approach angle prevents the ball carrier from cutting underneath the tackle attempt.

name: Two-High Drop Responsibilities
desc: LBs work their drop responsibilities behind a two-high safety shell — deeper drops to protect the intermediate crossing routes, tighter drops vs. a three-receiver surface to their side.

name: Linebacker Full Period
desc: Full linebacker position group period — run fits, pass coverage, blitz execution, and one-on-one coverage competition all in a single comprehensive period. Film every rep and review as a group.

name: React to Draw Play
desc: LBs practice reacting to a draw play after reading a pass drop by the QB — re-direct from coverage drop to a run fit on the draw. Slow-reacting LBs give up 10 yards on a draw every time.

name: LB in Dime Package
desc: One LB stays on the field in a dime package and works their coverage assignment — reduced run responsibility, increased coverage demand. Develop the LB's ability to play as a hybrid coverage player.

name: Linebacker vs. Jet Motion
desc: LBs adjust their gap responsibility and run key when a WR goes in jet motion — identify the jet sweep, adjust the box count, and ensure the outside gap is accounted for if the jet takes the handoff.

name: Wham Block Recognition
desc: LBs work recognizing a wham block from an H-back — the H-back comes from behind the line to block the LB. React to the wham early, press the blocker's outside shoulder, and shed toward the ball.

name: Strongside Linebacker vs. Power
desc: Strong-side LB works fitting the power run — identify the pulling guard as the key, set the correct gap, take the pulling guard head-on, and constrict the hole for the safety to make the tackle.

name: Linebacker Agility Circuit
desc: LB-specific agility circuit — mirror drill, shuffle-and-sprint, cone change-of-direction, and an angle-tackle finish. Circuit develops the total athleticism that makes linebackers valuable at both levels.

name: Big Blitz Package
desc: Install a big blitz — 6 or 7 rushers. LBs who blitz must take exact paths; LBs who drop must cover for the blitzing defenders. Full coordination required between the entire second and third levels.

name: Playing Through the Trash
desc: LBs work at the second level against blockers, debris, and downfield contact — pursue the ball carrier through bodies, pushing past interference, and finishing the tackle despite traffic.

name: Full Coverage Evaluation
desc: LBs are evaluated on their coverage ability — drop depth, hip turn, receiver matching, and ball skills. Coach grades each rep against 7-on-7 situations. Coverage performance tracked weekly.

name: Linebacker vs. Full Offense
desc: LBs work against a full offense in a team period — all run and pass assignments executed in real time against real formation diversity. The most game-realistic individual position-group drill available.

name: LB Spy on QB
desc: LB is assigned as a QB spy — their only job is to track the QB and prevent a scramble. Work the spy technique: stay between the QB and the first-down marker, don't chase receivers.

name: Scrape Exchange with DE
desc: DE crashes inside and LB scrapes outside on the same gap exchange. Timing of the exchange is the critical skill — LB must not be late or the gap is unaccounted for.

name: Hook Drop Depth
desc: LBs work dropping to the correct hook-zone depth — deep enough to take away 10-yard completions, shallow enough to be able to break on a 6-yard route. Hook depth is a judgment-based skill.

name: Leverage and Fit Cycle
desc: LBs cycle through leverage scenarios — playside fit, backside fit, and cutback fit — against a ball carrier running various paths from different formations. Develops full-field run-fit fluency.

name: LB vs. All Run Schemes
desc: LBs face every run scheme in the game plan — inside zone, outside zone, power, counter, trap — in a single rotating period. Each scheme tests a different LB responsibility and gap assignment.

name: Linebacker Communication Drill
desc: LBs work pre-snap communication as a group — Mike ID, front call, coverage rotation, and blitz package confirmation. The LB group must communicate a complete pre-snap picture on every play.

name: Scrape & Chase Drill
desc: LBs work the scrape-and-chase technique on an outside zone to their side — scrape flat down the line, read the cut-back, and redirect to chase the runner if he cuts back across the grain.

name: ILB Tandem Run Defense
desc: Both ILBs work tandem run defense — one fills the front gap, one cleans up the cutback. Coordination between the two ILBs ensures the defense has every gap covered regardless of the run direction.

name: LB Red Zone Assignments
desc: LBs work their red zone assignments — compressed coverage drops, tighter run fits with less field to work, and coverage against backs and TEs in the end zone. Red zone LB play wins championship games.

name: LB Competition Period
desc: Full LB competition period — one-on-one coverage reps, run-fit contests, and blitz execution all with win-loss tracking. Public scoreboard creates the urgency needed to develop at the position.

name: Zone Coverage Rotation
desc: DBs work coverage rotation calls — rotating from a two-high shell to a single-high look on a post-snap signal. Each DB adjusts their zone depth and responsibility without hesitation.

name: Man Coverage Hips Drill
desc: CB works hip turn in man coverage — backpedal to open-hip transition when the receiver breaks vertically. The hip turn must be smooth and immediate, not hesitant, to stay on top of the receiver.

name: Press Technique Footwork
desc: CB works press footwork — inside shade alignment, punch technique to redirect the receiver's release, and immediate hip turn to run. Footwork is the foundation of every successful press rep.

name: Safety Blitz Path
desc: Safety works a blitz path from depth — disguise in a two-high shell, trigger downhill at the snap, and take a straight-line path to the quarterback's set position. Time the blitz with the snap count.

name: DB Ball Skills Circuit
desc: DBs practice ball skills in a circuit — high-point jump balls, catch over the shoulder, PBU hand technique, and one-handed interception. Elite DBs win the ball as well as covering receivers.

name: Corner vs. Slot WR
desc: Corner works one-on-one against a slot receiver — inside alignment, shorter release distance, faster route breaks. The slot matchup requires adjustments in depth and timing from the corner's boundary technique.

name: Cover 1 Free Man
desc: Free safety works Cover 1 responsibilities — center-field alignment, read the QB's eyes, trigger on the throw, and arrive at the catch point before the receiver. Free man safety is the last line of defense.

name: Interception Return Drill
desc: DBs practice the full interception sequence — track the ball, make the catch, tuck immediately, and return. Practice the turn-and-run move after the interception. Every INT is a scoring opportunity.

name: Two-High Safety Communication
desc: Free and strong safety work two-high communication — confirm the coverage call, identify the hash, call out route threats, and rotate on a single-high trigger call. Pre-snap communication saves touchdowns.

name: Cover 3 Corner Technique
desc: Corner works Cover 3 bail technique — open inside foot at the snap, gain depth, and take the deep third. Work vs. a double move — don't bite on the first break. Deep third is non-negotiable.

name: DB Tackling Circuit
desc: DBs work tackling in a circuit — open-field breaks, run-support fits, and sideline tackles. DBs must be functional tacklers; missed tackles in the secondary result in big plays.

name: Flat-Footed Break Drill
desc: DB works breaking on the ball from a flat-footed position — hold the backpedal, read the QB's shoulder turn, and fire to the catch point without false-stepping. React to the ball, not the receiver.

name: Safety Run Support
desc: Safety works run support from a deep alignment — read the run, trigger downhill, take the correct angle, and make the tackle in space. The safety's run support determines whether runs break for long gains.

name: Over-the-Top Help
desc: Safety works over-the-top help on a post or seam route — read the route's depth, rotate over the top at the correct time, and arrive at the catch point simultaneously with the ball.

name: Inside Leverage Man
desc: CB works inside leverage in man coverage — force the receiver outside against the sideline. Stay inside the receiver's frame, turn and run on vertical, and use the sideline as a second defender.

name: DB Versus Double Move
desc: CB works against a double move — must not jump the first break, stay level in the backpedal, and react to the second break with an immediate burst. Discipline in the first phase prevents the deep ball.

name: Zone Match Drop
desc: DBs work a zone-match drop — start in zone alignment, then match a receiver who enters their zone as if in man. Zone-match coverage combines zone alignment with man-coverage execution.

name: Cornerback Trail Coverage
desc: CB works trailing a receiver at hip depth — one arm's length behind, inside leverage, turn and locate ball at the last moment. Trail technique prevents separation without aggressive press.

name: Safety Quarters Coverage
desc: Both safeties work quarters (Cover 4) responsibilities — each safety takes the deep quarter on their side. Against a number-two vertical, the safety squeezes; against a crossing route, the safety rotates to help.

name: DB Alignment vs. Formations
desc: DBs practice aligning correctly to every offensive formation — trips, bunch, empty, and compressed. Correct alignment pre-snap determines which coverage can be executed on every snap.

name: Corner Versus Fade
desc: CB works against a fade route — inside leverage, turn and run with the receiver, locate the ball over the outside shoulder, and contest at the highest point. Fade coverage is a red zone staple.

name: Free Safety Post Read
desc: Free safety reads the post route threat — if a receiver breaks on a post pattern, the FS triggers toward the deep middle hash and arrives to contest or tackle. Center-field range is the FS's premier attribute.

name: DB Leverage and Shade
desc: DBs work playing various leverage positions — inside shade, outside shade, and head-up — and the route stems that each shade is designed to stop. Pre-snap shade is the first coverage technique.

name: Nickel DB Versus Slot
desc: Nickel DB works all responsibilities — press the slot, zone drop to the hook-curl, and blitz from the nickel slot. The nickel position is the most versatile in the secondary.

name: DB Film Breakdown
desc: DBs review film from the last game together — identify releases that beat them, double moves that worked, and communication breakdowns between the safeties. Film study is the fastest path to improvement.

name: Cover 2 Corner Assignment
desc: Cover 2 corner works their three-stage assignment — play the flat route at 5 yards, pass it off to the hook defender, then reroute to carry the deep outside third. The Cover 2 corner does three jobs on one play.

name: Catch the Ball First
desc: DBs work the fundamental catch-first technique on deflections and interceptions — two hands, soft hands, secure the ball before thinking about the return. Dropped interceptions are the most costly secondary mistake.

name: Defensive Back Rerouting
desc: DBs practice rerouting receivers at the line of scrimmage without a penalty — redirect the release path, disrupt the timing of the route, and recover into coverage. Legal contact technique within 5 yards.

name: Strong Safety Run Force
desc: Strong safety works run force from a box alignment — read the TE's block or release, trigger downhill on a run block, and set the edge from the secondary level. Force means the runner turns back inside.

name: Crack Replace Responsibility
desc: DB works the crack-replace technique — when a WR cracks down on the SS, the CB comes underneath and takes over the force responsibility. Crack-replace prevents a blocked edge on perimeter runs.

name: DB vs. All Route Tree
desc: CBs work against a WR running every route from the route tree — one route per rep. Reading the WR's stems, maintaining leverage, and recovering from a beaten position are all practiced in the same period.

name: DB Competitive Catch Drill
desc: DBs and WRs compete on every throw — no coverage rules, just competition for the ball. Pure ball skills and physicality in contested catch situations build the competitive edge of the secondary.

name: Zone Drop Landmarks
desc: DBs work their zone drop landmarks — specific yard lines and hash marks they must reach in their drop before the ball is thrown. Consistent landmarks create consistent coverage depth across all zone calls.

name: Safety Force vs. Option
desc: Safeties work force responsibility against option plays — the safety is the pitch-force defender. Read the pitch key, take on the blocker, and force the pitch wide or make the tackle on the pitch man.

name: Cover 3 Sky vs. Cloud
desc: DBs work the Cover 3 sky (safety rotates to flat) and cloud (corner rotates to flat) variations. The corner or safety must recognize their rotation responsibility based on the play-side call.

name: DB Pursuit Drill
desc: DBs work pursuit of a ball carrier running away from them — take the correct over-the-top angle, accelerate, and make the tackle from behind. No plays off; every DB pursues every ball carrier.

name: Boundary vs. Field Corner
desc: Work the different responsibilities of a boundary corner vs. a field corner — different leverage, different depth, and different route threats based on the field's width on each side.

name: CB Bump and Run Technique
desc: CB works full bump-and-run technique — press alignment at zero cushion, punch the receiver's release, redirect inside or force outside, and turn to run with no cushion given. Aggressive man coverage.

name: Secondary Rotation Communication
